text: The surgeon begins the intervention by performing medial control of the uterine vessels at their origin. The posterior phase of the broad ligaments is opened, allowing gas to expand the ligaments, facilitating the spotting and dissection of the ureter. An incision is made, revealing two contiguous conduits: an external vascular conduit and an internal urinary conduit. The ureter is observed running parallel to the inner iliac artery in the retro-ligamentary portion.

The surgeon carefully rotates and gradually detaches the ureter from the inner iliac artery using progressive movements. The internal iliac artery is visible, and as the dissection progresses caudally, the uterine artery is identified. The ureter is clearly seen crossing the uterine artery, and a vessel detaching from the inner iliac artery is observed pulsating against the ureter.

The surgeon clips the uterine artery under visual control, ensuring no risk. The procedure is mirrored on the left side with the opening of the left large ligament. The instrumentalist holds the scissors while the surgeon treats the cranial anex to update the posterior phase of the large ligament. Two lateral contiguous conduits are identified: the internal one as the ureter and the external one suspected to be the internal arteriole. The surgeon checks the vessel by pulling on the umbilical artery to confirm its identity, then clips the uterine artery lower than usual.

The uterine vein is visible below as the surgeon performs a myomectomy using bipolar forceps and scissors. A vertical incision is made, approximately sixty to seventy percent of the uterine volume, to remove a fibroma measuring about seventy millimeters. The incision is around five centimeters, and the fibrous tracts binding the fibroma to the uterus are treated divergently. Coagulation is applied to the fibroma, avoiding the myometrium, and the fibroma is gradually separated from the uterus. Hemostasis is achieved quickly with sutures.

The surgeon uses Bicryl No. I and Gumil-Carran sutures to invaginate the hysterotomy scar, incorporating part of the myometrium into the fibrous crater to reduce hematoma risk. The sutures are placed to ensure a fully peritoneal hysterotomy scar, minimizing postoperative adhesion risk. Atracorporeal nodes are used for better tightening, with the thread blocked immediately to complete the sutures. The surgeon carefully places two or three sutures, avoiding multiple punctures to prevent bleeding and preserve the myometrial wall integrity.

The surgeon places a final stitch to secure the top of the pleat, ensuring the needle does not pierce the right or left tubular ostium to prevent infertility. The fibroma is chiseled and the chisel is set to eight hundred turns per minute, placed in the left foscilia. The ureter is visible, passing freely under the clipped uterine artery, with the clips left in place. The uterine artery is clipped, and the surgical field is washed.
